citations.narrative:
At the time of the inspection, there were several enclosures with exposed gridwall, metal framework - with approximately
4 x 4 inch panels - overlying the dirt of the entire enclosure floor, whereby there were large gaps both between and
underneath the framework of the following animals: African Crested Porcupines (one male and one female), Patagonian
Cavy's (two males), one racoon, Fennec foxes (one male and one female), and Bat Eared foxes (one male and one
female). In the porcupine enclosure, there was also a single row of exposed sharp points of metal (protruding
approximately 4 inches from the cement lining the perimeter floor of the enclosure) overlying the dirt of the entire front wall
of the enclosure. Sharp points, exposed metal framework, as well as the surrounding gaps/holes (some of which were dirt
holes dug by the animals) pose an entrapment/injury risk to the animals contained therein. The indoor and outdoor
housing facilities must be structurally sound and must be maintained in good repair to protect the animals from injury.
Correct by: February 24, 2026
This inspection was a site approval inspection.
This site is not ready for approval to conduct activity covered under your current Animal Welfare Act License.
Conducting regulated activities at a non-approved site is a violation of the Animal Welfare Act.
Please contact your inspector to schedule your next new site approval inspection.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the licensee.
n

narrative:
3.125(a) 
   
Facilities, general. 
At the time of the inspection, there were several enclosures with exposed gridwall, metal framework - with approximately 
4 x 4 inch panels - overlying the dirt of the entire enclosure floor, whereby there were large gaps both between and 
underneath the framework of the following animals: African Crested Porcupines (one male and one female), Patagonian 
Cavy's (two males), one racoon, Fennec foxes (one male and one female), and Bat Eared foxes (one male and one 
female). In the porcupine enclosure, there was also a single row of exposed sharp points of metal (protruding 
approximately 4 inches from the cement lining the perimeter floor of the enclosure) overlying the dirt of the entire front wall 
of the enclosure. Sharp points, exposed metal framework, as well as the surrounding gaps/holes (some of which were dirt 
holes dug by the animals) pose an entrapment/injury risk to the animals contained therein. The indoor and outdoor 
housing facilities must be structurally sound and must be maintained in good repair to protect the animals from injury. 
 
Correct by: February 24, 2026 
 
This inspection was a site approval inspection. 
 
This site is not ready for approval to conduct activity covered under your current Animal Welfare Act License. 
 
Conducting regulated activities at a non-approved site is a violation of the Animal Welfare Act. 
 
Please contact your inspector to schedule your next new site approval inspection. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the licensee.
